Pack a set of rectangles into a bounding box with minimum area

# Welcome to rectangle-packer

{{ PyPI-Versions }} {{ PyPI-Wheel }} {{ Build-Status }} {{ Read-the-Docs }} {{ GitHub-License }}

{{ PyPI-Downloads }}

**Primary use:** Given a set of rectangles with fixed orientations,
find a bounding box of minimum area that contains them all with no
overlap.

This project is inspired by Matt Perdeck's blog post [Fast Optimizing
Rectangle Packing Algorithm for Building CSS Sprites][fast optimizing rectangle packing algorithm for building css sprites].

- The latest documentation is available on [Read the Docs].
- The source code is available on [GitHub].

## Installation

Install latest version from [PyPI]:

```sh
$ python3 -m pip install rectangle-packer
```

Or [clone the repository] and install with:

```sh
$ python3 setup.py install
```

## Basic usage

```python
# Import the module
>>> import rpack

# Create a bunch of rectangles (width, height)
>>> sizes = [(58, 206), (231, 176), (35, 113), (46, 109)]

# Pack
>>> positions = rpack.pack(sizes)

# The result will be a list of (x, y) positions:
>>> positions
[(0, 0), (58, 0), (289, 0), (289, 113)]
```

The output positions are the lower left corner coordinates of each
rectangle in the input.

These positions will yield a packing with no overlaps and enclosing
area as small as possible (best effort).

:::{note}
- You must use **positive integers** as rectangle width and height.
- The module name is **rpack** which is an abbreviation of the package
  name at PyPI (rectangle-packer).
- The computational time required by `rpack.pack()` increases by
  the number *and* size of input rectangles. If this becomes a problem,
  you might need to implement your own [divide-and-conquer algorithm].
:::

## Examples

**Example A:**

```{image} https://penlect.com/rpack/2.0.1/img/packing_best_10.png
:align: center
:alt: pack10
```

**Example B:**

```{image} https://penlect.com/rpack/2.0.1/img/packing_phi.png
:align: center
:alt: packphi
```

**Example C:** Sometimes the input rectangles simply cannot be packed in
a good way. Here is an example of low packing density:

```{image} https://penlect.com/rpack/2.0.1/img/packing_worst_10.png
:align: center
:alt: pack10bad
```

**Example D:** The image below is contributed by Paul Brodersen, and
illustrates a solution to a problem discussed at [stackoverflow].

```{image} https://i.stack.imgur.com/kLat8.png
:align: center
:alt: PaulBrodersenExampleImage
:width: 450px
```

[clone the repository]: https://github.com/Penlect/rectangle-packer
[divide-and-conquer algorithm]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divide-and-conquer_algorithm
[fast optimizing rectangle packing algorithm for building css sprites]: http://www.codeproject.com/Articles/210979/Fast-optimizing-rectangle-packing-algorithm-for-bu
[github]: https://github.com/Penlect/rectangle-packer
[pypi]: https://pypi.org/project/rectangle-packer/
[read the docs]: https://rectangle-packer.readthedocs.io/en/latest/
[stackoverflow]: https://stackoverflow.com/a/53156709/2912349

modules.name: test.test_memory
documentation:
Search for memory leaks in rectangle-packer.

Since this project involves C code there is always a risk of bugs
causing memory leaks. This module uses memory_profiler to check
memory usage over time.

<https://pypi.org/project/memory-profiler/>

Run this command:

```
$ mprof run python3 -m test.test_memory; and mprof plot
```

functions.name: rpack.pack
documentation:
Pack rectangles into a bounding box with minimal area.

The result is returned as a list of coordinates "(x, y)", which
specifices the location of each corresponding input rectangle's
lower left corner.

The helper function {py:func}`bbox_size` can be used to compute
the width and height of the resulting bounding box. And
{py:func}`packing_density` can be used to evaluate the packing
quality.

The algorithm will sort the input in different ways internally so
there is no need to sort `sizes` in advance.

The GIL is released when C-intensive code is running. Execution
time increases by the number *and* size of input rectangles. If
this becomes a problem, you might need to implement your own
[divide-and-conquer algorithm].

**Example**:

```
# Import the module
>>> import rpack

# Create a bunch of rectangles (width, height)
>>> sizes = [(58, 206), (231, 176), (35, 113), (46, 109)]

# Pack
>>> positions = rpack.pack(sizes)

# The result will be a list of (x, y) positions:
>>> positions
[(0, 0), (58, 0), (289, 0), (289, 113)]
```

```
param sizes

"(width, height)" of the rectangles to pack.  Note:
integer values only!

type sizes

Iterable[Tuple[int, int]]

param max_width

Force the enclosing rectangle to not exceed a
maximum width.  If not possible,
 will be raised.

type max_width

Union[None, int]

param max_height

Force the enclosing rectangle to not exceed a
maximum height.  If not possible,
 will be raised.

type max_height

Union[None, int]

return

List of positions (x, y) of the input rectangles.

rtype

List[Tuple[int, int]]
```

[divide-and-conquer algorithm]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divide-and-conquer_algorithm

modules.name: misc.crunch
documentation:
Benchmark rpack package

This module test rpack performance and packing density quality.

These are the performance tests:

* Uniform side length test
* Sequence of squares test
* Uniform area test (todo)

# TODO Square-packing problem NxN

functions.name: misc.crunch.rectangles_square
documentation: Return `n` square rectangles from (1, 1) to (n, n)

functions.name: misc.crunch.rectangles_circum
documentation: Return `n` fixed circumference rectangles, w + h = n

functions.name: misc.crunch.rectangles_unif_side
documentation: Return list of `n` rec. with random side lengths `unif{0, m}`

functions.name: misc.crunch.rectangles_unif_area
documentation: Return list of `n` rec. with random area `unif{0, m}`

methods.name: test.test_pack.TestPackOutput.test_perfect_pack
documentation:
Pack rectangles to perfect rectangle

Like this:

```
aaa  bb  cc  -->  aaabb
aaa  bb           aaabb
aaa               aaacc
```
